## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness ŌĆö fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly ŌĆö if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; highŌåÆmax: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only ŌĆö it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 ŌåÆ 4.6, 4.6 ŌåÆ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Mostly mechanical fixture extension, gated on one design judgment (below).
  
  ## Status correction (verified 260527)
  
  Deliverable A ŌĆö the actionable-failure repair ŌĆö is **already satisfied** by
  prior heat work; the docket's original "confirmed live crash" premise is stale.
  Verified: the OAuth render fails actionably when credentials are absent
  ("RBRO credentials missing (ŌĆ”) - run rbgp_payor_install", exit 1), reproduced
  by renaming the payor credential file aside and restoring it. Survey of every
  render/validate/info shows each either delegates to the safe `buv_render`/
  `buv_report` engine (which uses `${!var:-}` indirect expansion) or carries a
  furnish-level `buc_die`/sentinel guard. No raw `set -u` death path remains.
  Re-confirm with a spot reproduction before assuming, but expect no repair work.
  
  ## The work ŌĆö fixture coverage (was Deliverable C)
  
  The fast theurge fixture's regime-smoke set omits several renders/infos. Add the
  omitted ones so every render/validate/info is exercised by the fast fixture.
  
  Discovery recipe for the gap: list the `rbtdrf_rs_*` cases registered in the
  regime-smoke `case!` block against the full set of `*_render`/`*_validate`/
  `*_info` definitions under `Tools/buk` + `Tools/rbk`. The difference is the
  work ŌĆö expect the credential-gated (OAuth), folio-gated (auth roles), station,
  depot-render, the BUK node/privilege/peer regimes, and the bottle info path.
  
  ## Locked design question ŌĆö resolve before coding the gated cases
  
  Credential-gated renders (OAuth) exit 0 only when the credential is present, so
  a clean-exit assertion passes on a configured station but fails in a
  credential-free context. The fast suite is the no-dependencies tier. Decide per
  gated render: stage the prerequisite, or assert the actionable-failure path
  (the fixture already has a negative-exit helper). This is the judgment the pace
  turns on ŌĆö do not silently assume creds are always present.
  
  ## Done
  
  Every regime render/validate/info is invoked by the fast fixture; each gated
  render has a deliberate, documented assertion stance (clean-exit-when-staged or
  actionable-failure-when-absent).
  
  ## References
  
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` ŌĆö fixture to extend (regime-smoke `case!` block)
  - `Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh` ŌĆö safe `buv_render` engine
  - `Tools/rbk/rbro_cli.sh`, `rbro_regime.sh` ŌĆö the already-actionable OAuth path (reference pattern)

  ## Character
  
  Mostly mechanical fixture extension, gated on one design judgment (below).
  
  ## Status correction (verified 260527)
  
  Deliverable A ŌĆö the actionable-failure repair ŌĆö is **already satisfied** by
  prior heat work; the docket's original "confirmed live crash" premise is stale.
  Verified: the OAuth render fails actionably when credentials are absent
  ("RBRO credentials missing (ŌĆ”) - run rbgp_payor_install", exit 1), reproduced
  by renaming the payor credential file aside and restoring it. Survey of every
  render/validate/info shows each either delegates to the safe `buv_render`/
  `buv_report` engine (which uses `${!var:-}` indirect expansion) or carries a
  furnish-level `buc_die`/sentinel guard. No raw `set -u` death path remains.
  Re-confirm with a spot reproduction before assuming, but expect no repair work.
  
  ## The work ŌĆö fixture coverage (was Deliverable C)
  
  The fast theurge fixture's regime-smoke set omits several renders/infos. Add the
  omitted ones so every render/validate/info is exercised by the fast fixture.
  
  Discovery recipe for the gap: list the `rbtdrf_rs_*` cases registered in the
  regime-smoke `case!` block against the full set of `*_render`/`*_validate`/
  `*_info` definitions under `Tools/buk` + `Tools/rbk`. The difference is the
  work ŌĆö expect the credential-gated (OAuth), folio-gated (auth roles), station,
  depot-render, the BUK node/privilege/peer regimes, and the bottle info path.
  
  ## Locked design question ŌĆö resolve before coding the gated cases
  
  Credential-gated renders (OAuth) exit 0 only when the credential is present, so
  a clean-exit assertion passes on a configured station but fails in a
  credential-free context. The fast suite is the no-dependencies tier. Decide per
  gated render: stage the prerequisite, or assert the actionable-failure path
  (the fixture already has a negative-exit helper). This is the judgment the pace
  turns on ŌĆö do not silently assume creds are always present.
  
  ## Done
  
  Every regime render/validate/info is invoked by the fast fixture; each gated
  render has a deliberate, documented assertion stance (clean-exit-when-staged or
  actionable-failure-when-absent).
  
  ## References
  
  - `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` ŌĆö fixture to extend (regime-smoke `case!` block)

1	## Paddock: rbk-14-mvp-release-finalize
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	A small cleanup-and-validation heat at the tail of the Recipe Bottle release
6	effort. Its original charter ŌĆö supply-chain trust hardening plus
7	release-qualification tooling ŌĆö has largely shipped or migrated. The
8	release-qualification charter now lives in the rbk-16 heat (ŌéŻBB): the
9	marshal-zero ŌåÆ gauntlet/pristine model, the RELEASE.md runbook, and the
10	prep-release ceremony all belong there. The prep-release ceremony rewrite and
11	the foundry/cloud-step trust audit that once lived here were restrung into ŌéŻBB
12	so the release ceremony and the foundry surface each have a single owner.
13	
14	What remains in ŌéŻAU is genuinely local: developer-facing hygiene and end-to-end
15	validation that do not depend on the gauntlet model.
16	
17	## Remaining Scope
18	
19	- **Regime-render error hygiene** ŌĆö every regime render/validate/info function
20	  should fail with an actionable parameter message, not a terse "module not
21	  kindled" crash or a set -u unbound-variable death. Includes a sweep test
22	  fixture that exercises every render/info and asserts clean exit, plus logging
23	  discipline for batch-sweep context.
24	- **Onboarding end-to-end walk** ŌĆö exercise every handbook track in sequence
25	  from a learner's perspective; confirm windows render with prefixed resource
26	  names and probe outputs report the correct prefixed identifiers.
27	- **openssl base64 helper factoring** ŌĆö the six base64 sites that ŌéŻBB's base64
28	  sweep unified on openssl still carry a duplicated invocation; factor into
29	  rbgu_Utility.sh helpers, or close won't-fix with rationale recorded.
30	- **Small doc/file hygiene** ŌĆö relocate the compose-probe sentinel out of the
31	  .rbk/ runtime config directory into Tools/rbk/ (the file move itself already
32	  appears done ŌĆö verify references before closing); and a BCG clarification
33	  distinguishing the `$(<file)` builtin redirect from command substitution
34	  (verify against the landed substitution-rule refinement first ŌĆö may already
35	  be covered).
36	
37	## Relationship to other heats
38	
39	- **ŌéŻBB (rbk-16-mvp-release-qualification)** ŌĆö the live release-qualification
40	  heat; inherited this heat's prep-release ceremony work and the foundry trust
41	  audit. The audit is sequenced at ŌéŻBB's tail so it reviews the post-reshaping
42	  foundry surface rather than transitional code.
43	- **ŌéŻAk (rbk-mvp-1, retired)** ŌĆö prior art for the directory/spec/test
44	  structure.
45	
46	## Deferred technical debt (post-release itch candidates)
47	
48	Catalogued during this heat's BCG-compliance work; deferred, not lost. Better
49	homed in jji_itch.md than in the paddock long-term:
50	
51	- `z_` prefix on locals ŌĆö naming convention, no runtime impact (pervasive)
52	- Unbraced `"$var"` expansions ŌĆö mechanical
53	- `2>/dev/null` stderr suppression ŌĆö case-by-case judgment
54	- `local -r` missing on single-assignment locals ŌĆö pervasive
55	- Raw `echo` for user messages in display-heavy modules
56	- Bare file truncation (`> file` without `:`)
57	
58	## References
59	
60	- Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh ŌĆö qualification orchestrator
61	- Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh ŌĆö core `buv_render()` engine
62	- Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md ŌĆö command-substitution rules
63	- Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh ŌĆö utility home for the base64 helpers
64	- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS0-SpecTop.adoc ŌĆö main specification

file.content:
## Paddock: rbk-14-mvp-release-finalize

## Context

A small cleanup-and-validation heat at the tail of the Recipe Bottle release
effort. Its original charter ŌĆö supply-chain trust hardening plus
release-qualification tooling ŌĆö has largely shipped or migrated. The
release-qualification charter now lives in the rbk-16 heat (ŌéŻBB): the
marshal-zero ŌåÆ gauntlet/pristine model, the RELEASE.md runbook, and the
prep-release ceremony all belong there. The prep-release ceremony rewrite and
the foundry/cloud-step trust audit that once lived here were restrung into ŌéŻBB
so the release ceremony and the foundry surface each have a single owner.

What remains in ŌéŻAU is genuinely local: developer-facing hygiene and end-to-end
validation that do not depend on the gauntlet model.

## Remaining Scope

- **Regime-render error hygiene** ŌĆö every regime render/validate/info function
  should fail with an actionable parameter message, not a terse "module not
  kindled" crash or a set -u unbound-variable death. Includes a sweep test
  fixture that exercises every render/info and asserts clean exit, plus logging
  discipline for batch-sweep context.
- **Onboarding end-to-end walk** ŌĆö exercise every handbook track in sequence
  from a learner's perspective; confirm windows render with prefixed resource
  names and probe outputs report the correct prefixed identifiers.
- **openssl base64 helper factoring** ŌĆö the six base64 sites that ŌéŻBB's base64
  sweep unified on openssl still carry a duplicated invocation; factor into
  rbgu_Utility.sh helpers, or close won't-fix with rationale recorded.
- **Small doc/file hygiene** ŌĆö relocate the compose-probe sentinel out of the
  .rbk/ runtime config directory into Tools/rbk/ (the file move itself already
  appears done ŌĆö verify references before closing); and a BCG clarification
  distinguishing the `$(<file)` builtin redirect from command substitution
  (verify against the landed substitution-rule refinement first ŌĆö may already
  be covered).

## Relationship to other heats

- **ŌéŻBB (rbk-16-mvp-release-qualification)** ŌĆö the live release-qualification
  heat; inherited this heat's prep-release ceremony work and the foundry trust
  audit. The audit is sequenced at ŌéŻBB's tail so it reviews the post-reshaping
  foundry surface rather than transitional code.
- **ŌéŻAk (rbk-mvp-1, retired)** ŌĆö prior art for the directory/spec/test
  structure.

## Deferred technical debt (post-release itch candidates)

Catalogued during this heat's BCG-compliance work; deferred, not lost. Better
homed in jji_itch.md than in the paddock long-term:

- `z_` prefix on locals ŌĆö naming convention, no runtime impact (pervasive)
- Unbraced `"$var"` expansions ŌĆö mechanical
- `2>/dev/null` stderr suppression ŌĆö case-by-case judgment
- `local -r` missing on single-assignment locals ŌĆö pervasive
- Raw `echo` for user messages in display-heavy modules
- Bare file truncation (`> file` without `:`)

## References

- Tools/rbk/rbq_Qualify.sh ŌĆö qualification orchestrator
- Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh ŌĆö core `buv_render()` engine
- Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md ŌĆö command-substitution rules
- Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh ŌĆö utility home for the base64 helpers
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS0-SpecTop.adoc ŌĆö main specification

content.text:
## Mount: ŌéóAUAAh ŌĆö prerelease-regime-render-sweep

**Goal in one sentence:** Extend the fast theurge fixture's `regime-smoke` case set so that *every* regime render/validate/info command is exercised, with each credential- or folio-gated command given a deliberate, documented assertion stance (clean-exit-when-staged vs. actionable-failure-when-absent).

**Heat context:** ŌéŻAU (rbk-14-mvp-release-finalize) ŌĆö the local tail-end hygiene/validation heat; the release-qualification charter migrated to ŌéŻBB. 41/43 done. Recent work was the openssl base64 factoring and a BCG builtin-redirect clarification.

**Important status note from the docket:** Deliverable A (the actionable-failure repair) is *already satisfied* ŌĆö verified 260527 that OAuth render fails actionably when creds are absent, and no raw `set -u` death path remains. So this pace is **fixture coverage only**, not repair work.

### What I found (discovery)

Current `RBTDRF_CASES_REGIME_SMOKE` registers 9 cases:
`burc`, `burs`, `rbrn`, `rbrr`, `rbrr_nonempty_prefix`, `rbrv`, `rbrp`, `burd`, `unmake_empty_arg_refusal`.

Cross-referencing against the full render/validate/info surface, the **uncovered** commands match the docket's predicted gap:

| Gap | Commands | Gate type |
|-----|----------|-----------|
| OAuth | `rbw-ror` / `rbw-rov` | credential-gated |
| Auth roles | `rbw-rar` / `rbw-rav` / `rbw-ral` | folio-gated |
| Station | `rbw-rsr` / `rbw-rsv` | (verify vs. existing `burs`) |
| Depot | `rbw-rdr` / `rbw-rdv` | likely clean |
| BUK node/privilege/peer | (BUK-level regimes) | TBD |
| Bottle info | bottle info path | TBD |

### The locked design question ŌĆö this is what the pace turns on

For **credential-gated** (OAuth) and **folio-gated** (auth roles) renders, a clean-exit assertion passes on a configured station but *fails* in the no-dependency fast tier. The docket requires deciding **per gated render**: stage the prerequisite, or assert the actionable-failure path (the fixture already has a negative-exit helper). I need your stance before coding those cases.

questions.question: For the credential-gated (OAuth) and folio-gated (auth-roles) renders in the no-dependency fast tier, what assertion stance do you want?

options.label: Assert actionable-failure
options.description: For all gated renders, assert the actionable-failure path (use the existing negative-exit helper). Simplest, no staging, exercises the very error-hygiene path Deliverable A confirmed. Keeps the fast tier genuinely dependency-free.

options.label: Stage prerequisites
options.description: Synthesize minimal fixtures/credentials in the temp dir so each gated render exits 0 clean. More setup code; risks coupling the fast tier to credential shape.

options.label: Per-render judgment
options.description: Decide case-by-case ŌĆö stage where a synthetic prereq is cheap and meaningful, assert-failure where staging would be artificial. I'll propose a per-render table for your review before coding.

I need a complete, authoritative enumeration of every "regime" render / validate / info / list command in this repo, so I can verify which are covered by a test fixture.

Search across `Tools/buk/` and `Tools/rbk/` (and tabtargets in `tt/`).

Specifically:

1. **Tabtarget colophons**: List every tabtarget in `tt/` whose colophon is a regime render/validate/info/list command. Look for patterns like `rbw-r*` (e.g. rbw-rnr, rbw-rnv, rbw-rvr, rbw-rrr, rbw-rdr, rbw-rpr, rbw-ror, rbw-rsr, rbw-rar, rbw-ral, rbw-rdi, rbw-rdc), and BUK regime ones like `buw-rc*`, `buw-rs*`, and any node/privilege/peer regime colophons. Also info commands: `rbw-ni` (NameplateInfo), `rbw-di` (DepotInfo), and any bottle info path. For each, give the colophon and one-line purpose.

2. **The underlying functions**: grep for shell function definitions matching `*_render`, `*_validate`, `*_info`, `*_list` in `Tools/buk/*.sh` and `Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`. Report the function name + file for each regime-related one.

3. **BUK regimes specifically**: The terms "node", "privilege", "peer" regimes are mentioned. Find what BUK regime types exist (look in `Tools/buk/burc_*.sh`, `burs_*.sh`, and any other `bur*_regime.sh` / `bur*_cli.sh`). List each BUK regime type and its render/validate colophons/commands.

4. **Gating**: For each render command, note whether it appears credential-gated (requires a credential file like RBRO/OAuth, RBRA), folio-gated (requires a param1 argument like a role name or nameplate moniker), or ungated (renders standalone).

Report as a structured list. Cite file paths and line numbers. Do NOT modify anything. Be thorough ŌĆö I need the COMPLETE surface, this is the crux of the task.

toolUseResult.prompt:
I need a complete, authoritative enumeration of every "regime" render / validate / info / list command in this repo, so I can verify which are covered by a test fixture.

Search across `Tools/buk/` and `Tools/rbk/` (and tabtargets in `tt/`).

Specifically:

1. **Tabtarget colophons**: List every tabtarget in `tt/` whose colophon is a regime render/validate/info/list command. Look for patterns like `rbw-r*` (e.g. rbw-rnr, rbw-rnv, rbw-rvr, rbw-rrr, rbw-rdr, rbw-rpr, rbw-ror, rbw-rsr, rbw-rar, rbw-ral, rbw-rdi, rbw-rdc), and BUK regime ones like `buw-rc*`, `buw-rs*`, and any node/privilege/peer regime colophons. Also info commands: `rbw-ni` (NameplateInfo), `rbw-di` (DepotInfo), and any bottle info path. For each, give the colophon and one-line purpose.

2. **The underlying functions**: grep for shell function definitions matching `*_render`, `*_validate`, `*_info`, `*_list` in `Tools/buk/*.sh` and `Tools/rbk/**/*.sh`. Report the function name + file for each regime-related one.

3. **BUK regimes specifically**: The terms "node", "privilege", "peer" regimes are mentioned. Find what BUK regime types exist (look in `Tools/buk/burc_*.sh`, `burs_*.sh`, and any other `bur*_regime.sh` / `bur*_cli.sh`). List each BUK regime type and its render/validate colophons/commands.

4. **Gating**: For each render command, note whether it appears credential-gated (requires a credential file like RBRO/OAuth, RBRA), folio-gated (requires a param1 argument like a role name or nameplate moniker), or ungated (renders standalone).

Report as a structured list. Cite file paths and line numbers. Do NOT modify anything. Be thorough ŌĆö I need the COMPLETE surface, this is the crux of the task.

---

### PART 4: BUK REGIME TYPES

BUK defines 6 regime types:

1. **BURC** (Configuration) ŌĆö singleton file, ungated render/validate
2. **BURE** (Environment) ŌĆö ambient (env vars), ungated render/validate
3. **BURN** (Node) ŌĆö multi-instance per project, folio-gated render/validate, ungated list
4. **BURP** (Privilege) ŌĆö multi-instance per station-user, folio-gated render/validate, ungated list
5. **BURS** (Station) ŌĆö singleton file per developer, ungated render/validate

---

### PART 5: RBK REGIME TYPES (Derived)

RBK defines 8 regime types:

1. **RBRN** (Nameplate) ŌĆö multi-instance, folio-gated render/validate, ungated list/survey/audit
2. **RBRV** (Vessel) ŌĆö multi-instance per repo, folio-gated render/validate, ungated list
3. **RBRR** (Repo) ŌĆö singleton file, ungated render/validate
4. **RBRD** (Depot) ŌĆö singleton file, ungated render/validate, folio-gated inscribe/check
5. **RBRP** (Payor) ŌĆö singleton file, ungated render/validate
6. **RBRO** (OAuth) ŌĆö credential-gated file, credential-gated render/validate
7. **RBRS** (Station) ŌĆö singleton file, ungated render/validate
8. **RBRA** (Auth/Credential) ŌĆö multi-instance per role, folio-gated render/validate (3 roles), ungated list

---

=== rbgp_depot_info (rbw-di) head ===
rbgp_depot_info() {
  zrbgp_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Run egress posture checks against the live depot's tether and airgap worker pools"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_step 'Authenticate as Payor'
  local z_token
  z_token=$(zrbgp_authenticate_capture) || buc_die "Failed to authenticate as Payor via OAuth"

  local -r z_mason_email="${RBCC_account_mason}-${RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER}@${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}.${RBGC_SA_EMAIL_DOMAIN}"
  local -r z_tether_id="${RBDC_GCB_POOL_STEM}${RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_TETHER}"
  local -r z_airgap_id="${RBDC_GCB_POOL_STEM}${RBGC_POOL_SUFFIX_AIRGAP}"

  local -r z_tether_posture_check="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_tether_posture_check.sh"
  local -r z_airgap_posture_check="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgp_airgap_posture_check.sh"

  buc_step 'Write posture-check scripts'
  zrbgp_write_posture_check tether "${z_tether_posture_check}"
  zrbgp_write_posture_check airgap "${z_airgap_posture_check}"

  buc_step 'Probe tether pool posture'
  zrbgp_pool_posture_submit "${z_token}" "tether" "${z_tether_id}" "${z_mason_email}" "${z_tether_posture_check}"

  buc_step 'Probe airgap pool posture'
  zrbgp_pool_posture_submit "${z_token}" "airgap" "${z_airgap_id}" "${z_mason_email}" "${z_airgap_posture_check}"

  buc_success 'Posture checks complete'
}

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buym_yelp.sh"

######################################################################
# Command Functions

rbrs_validate() {
  buc_doc_brief "Validate RBRS station regime configuration via enrollment report"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_step "Validating RBRS station regime file: ${RBCC_rbrs_file}"
  buv_report RBRS "Station Regime"
  buc_step "RBRS station regime valid"
}

rbrs_render() {
  buc_doc_brief "Display diagnostic view of RBRS station regime configuration"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buv_render RBRS "RBRS - Recipe Bottle Station Regime" "${RBCC_rbrs_file}"
}

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zrbrs_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  local z_rbk_kit_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burd_regime.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/bupr_PresentationRegime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbcc_Constants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbrs_regime.sh"

  zbuv_kindle
  zburd_kindle

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buym_yelp.sh"

######################################################################
# Command Functions

rbrs_validate() {
  buc_doc_brief "Validate RBRS station regime configuration via enrollment report"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buc_step "Validating RBRS station regime file: ${RBCC_rbrs_file}"
  buv_report RBRS "Station Regime"
  buc_step "RBRS station regime valid"
}

rbrs_render() {
  buc_doc_brief "Display diagnostic view of RBRS station regime configuration"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buv_render RBRS "RBRS - Recipe Bottle Station Regime" "${RBCC_rbrs_file}"
}

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zrbrs_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  local z_rbk_kit_dir="${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burd_regime.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/bupr_PresentationRegime.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbcc_Constants.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbrs_regime.sh"

  zbuv_kindle
  zburd_kindle

content.text:
I've now studied the actual code surface, and the docket's framing **is materially stale**. Here's what I verified and where the opus-4.7 framing breaks down.

## What I verified

**The full render/validate/info surface** (37 commands across BUK + RBK), cross-referenced against the 9 `RBTDRF_CASES_REGIME_SMOKE` cases. Currently covered: BURC, BURS, RBRN (folio, discovered), RBRR, RBRV (folio, discovered), RBRP. Plus a kindle test and the `rbw-dU` refusal.

**Which config actually exists in a clean checkout** (`git ls-files`): committed are `burc.env`, `burn.env` (bujn-winpc), `burp.env` (bhyslop/bujn-winpc), `rbrd.env`, `rbrp.env`, `rbrr.env`, 6├Ś `rbrn.env`, 9├Ś `rbrv.env`. **Not committed anywhere:** any `rbrs.env` (station), `rbro` (OAuth), `rbra` (auth-role) credential files.

**What the "info" commands actually do** ŌĆö read the bodies:
- `rbgp_depot_info` (rbw-di): *"Authenticate as Payor via OAuth"* ŌåÆ submits Cloud Build posture jobs. **Live GCP.**
- `rbrn_survey`/`rbrn_audit` (rbw-ni/rbw-nv): fleet survey with GAR status + preflight. **Live GAR.**

## Staleness findings

1. **"every render/validate/*info*" is incoherent for the fast tier.** The info commands (`rbw-ni`, `rbw-nv`, `rbw-di`, `rbw-dl`) all authenticate to GCP or query GAR. They are structurally *service/crucible*-tier and **cannot** live in the no-dependency fast fixture. The docket's inclusion of "info" contradicts the tier's defining constraint.

2. **"BUK node/privilege/*peer* regimes" ŌĆö there is no peer regime.** BUK has BURE (environment), BURN (node), BURP (privilege). "peer" is wrong/stale terminology.

3. **BURP is station-*user*-coupled, not a clean add.** Its only committed investiture is `bhyslop/bujn-winpc`; it renders cleanly only on bhyslop's station. Same for RBRS (no committed config). These break fast-tier determinism if asserted clean-exit.

4. **"bottle info path"** ŌĆö no such regime render exists in the surface. Stale.

## The reframed design answer

The docket poses the gated stance as a free per-render judgment. But the fast tier has a hard **determinism contract**: a case must behave identically on bhyslop's configured station *and* on a clean CI checkout. That collapses the judgment into a config-availability rule:

| Config status | Regimes | Tier-safe stance |
|---|---|---|
| **Committed** | RBRD, BURN (node), BURE (ambient) | Clean-render add ŌĆö *not actually gated* |
| **Station-local, uncommitted** | RBRS, BURP | Assert **actionable-failure** (can't stage without coupling) |
| **Credentials, never committed** | RBRO, RBRA | Assert **actionable-failure** |

So your deferred question effectively resolves to: **assert actionable-failure** for everything genuinely gated ŌĆö staging real credentials/station config would couple the no-dependency tier and is off-limits. Only the committed-config regimes are clean adds.

## Proposed revised scope

- **Add clean render+validate cases:** RBRD (`rbw-rdr/rdv`), BURN (`buw-rnr/rnv`, discovered like RBRN), and BURE (`buw-rer/rev`) if it renders ambient cleanly.
- **Add actionable-failure cases:** RBRO, RBRA, RBRS, BURP ŌĆö each asserting the documented non-zero failure path via the existing negative-exit helper.
- **Drop "info" from scope** ŌĆö note explicitly that survey/audit/depot-info/depot-list are service-tier and out of scope for the fast fixture (candidate for a separate service-tier sweep, itch-worthy).
- **Done:** every *render/validate* in BUK+RBK is invoked by the fast fixture; committed-config regimes assert clean exit, uncommitted/credential/station-local regimes assert actionable-failure; info commands explicitly excluded with rationale.

This is a tighter, coherent, tier-respecting target versus the 4.7 "every render/validate/info" framing.

What would you like to clarify? I'm happy to dig further into any part of the analysis or reconsider the framing before we settle on how to proceed.

A few things I can imagine you might want to probe:

- **Whether the "info commands are out of scope" call is right** ŌĆö maybe you intended the sweep to span tiers (a fast portion + a service-tier portion), rather than dropping info entirely. I treated "fast fixture" literally as the no-dependency tier.
- **The determinism stance** ŌĆö whether you'd accept staging *synthetic* (non-secret) config for RBRS/BURP/RBRA to get real clean-render coverage, rather than only asserting actionable-failure. That's a real option I dismissed on "no coupling," but synthetic fixtures might be acceptable to you.
- **Whether BURE/BURN belong here at all** ŌĆö they're BUK-level, and you might want BUK regime coverage owned by the BUK self-test (`buw-st`) rather than the rbk theurge fast fixture.
- **Scope size** ŌĆö whether this should stay one pace or split.

What's on your mind?

content.text:
Backing all the way up. Here's what I found when I stopped trusting the docket and asked instead "do these tools actually help users configure regimes well?"

## The one fact that reframes everything

The existing regime-validation fixture's negative cases assert **verdict only** ŌĆö `expect-pass = false`. They prove validate *rejects* bad config. They do **not** assert anything about the *message* the user sees. `rbtdrf_rv_rbrn_missing_moniker` would pass whether the failure says *"RBRN_MONIKER unset ŌĆö set it in rbrn.env"* or just *"error"*. The only case in the whole file that checks message text is the depot-unmake refusal (it asserts the `rbw-dl` pointer is emitted).

So the thing your users actually need ŌĆö **"when I misconfigure, does the tool tell me what's wrong and how to fix it?"** ŌĆö is essentially untested. Coverage exists; *actionability* doesn't.

## Fresh merit assessment

Stripping the pace to its purpose ŌĆö regimes are config files users hand-author; render/validate/list are their feedback loop ŌĆö here's the honest ledger:

**Already guaranteed:** no crash/set-u death (Deliverable A); validate accepts-good/rejects-bad *by verdict* for the core four (rbrr, rbrd, rbrv, rbrn); render runs without crashing on live config for six regimes.

**Genuinely not guaranteed:**
- **Message actionability** ŌĆö does the failure name the offending field and point to the fix? Untested everywhere.
- **Render readability** ŌĆö render is a *diagnostic view for the user*; nothing checks it's informative (resolved values shown, secrets masked).
- A few regimes (rbrp, rbrs, burc, burn, burp, rbro, rbra) have no validate-failure test at all.

**My judgment on the pace as written:** thin merit. "Invoke every render/validate/info once, assert clean-exit-or-actionable-failure" mostly re-proves "doesn't crash" (done) and adds invocation cases whose verdict-only assertion doesn't touch what users care about. That's coverage theater ŌĆö it fails the load-bearing-complexity test in CLAUDE.md. And "info" is incoherent for the fast tier anyway (those commands hit GCP/GAR).

## Where the real value is

If this pace is worth keeping, the high-merit version is **not** more invocation coverage ŌĆö it's a **message-actionability guarantee** for the regimes users actually author by hand. Concretely: for each such regime, drive a representative misconfiguration and assert the failure message *names the field and points to the remedy* ŌĆö and where a message is currently weak, **improve the tool**, with the test as the lock. That's "make the tools help users," not "prove the tools run."

The credential regimes (oauth/auth) are lower value for a release-finalize heat ŌĆö they're installed, not hand-authored. Info/survey is service-tier, out of scope.

## So I see three honest directions

1. **Reframe to the actionability guarantee** ŌĆö audit each hand-authored regime's validate/render *message*, improve the weak ones, lock with message-asserting tests. Smaller, sharper, genuinely user-facing.
2. **Shrink to a verification + itch** ŌĆö confirm Deliverable A holds across all regimes (spot reproductions), record that validation-logic coverage is sufficient, and file an itch for the message-actionability audit as post-release polish. Closes the heat tail cheaply.
3. **Drop with rationale** ŌĆö if the validation engine's actionability is already trusted in practice, retire the pace and note why.

I lean toward **#1 if you want lasting user value here, #2 if release-finalize wants to stay lean.** I don't think the pace as written earns its place.

Which direction fits how you're thinking about the release ŌĆö and do you have a read on whether the validate *messages* are actually good today, or is that an open question worth me sampling a few?

message.content: #1 is definitely the intent.  What choices would help us reach this goal?

content.text: Good ŌĆö #1 it is. Before I lay out the choices, one of them hinges on a fact I should check: *how* validate reports failures today. If it already emits structured, field-level diagnostics, the test assertions (and the actionability bar) look very different than if it just prints prose. Let me look at the `buv_report`/`buv_render` engine and the yelp wire format.

content.content:
=== buv_report + buv_render: what do they emit on failure? ===
713:buv_report() {
794:buv_render() {
--- buv_report body ---
buv_report() {
  zbuv_sentinel

  local z_scope="${1:-}"
  local z_label="${2:-}"
  test -n "${z_scope}" || buc_die "buv_report: scope required"
  test -n "${z_label}" || buc_die "buv_report: label required"

  local z_any_failed=0

  buc_step "${z_label}"

  local z_i
  for z_i in "${!z_buv_scope_roll[@]}"; do
    test "${z_buv_scope_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_scope}" || continue

    local z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_i]}"
    local z_type="${z_buv_type_roll[$z_i]}"
    local z_val="${!z_varname:-}"
    local z_err

    # Secret redaction ŌĆö replace display value before PASS/FAIL output
    local z_display_val="${z_val}"
    if test "${z_type}" = "secret" && test -n "${z_val}"; then
      z_display_val="(redacted ŌĆö ${#z_val} chars)"
    fi

    z_err=$(zbuv_check_capture "${z_i}")
    if test -z "${z_err}"; then
      buc_step "  PASS  ${z_varname}=${z_display_val} [${z_type}]"
    elif test "${z_err}" = "${BUV_check_gated}"; then
      buc_step "  SKIP  ${z_varname} (gated)"
    else
      buc_step "  FAIL  ${z_varname}=${z_display_val} [${z_type}]: ${z_err#"${BUV_check_fail}"}"
      z_any_failed=1
    fi
  done

  return "${z_any_failed}"
}

content.content:
=== buv_render body ===
buv_render() {
  zbuv_sentinel
  zbupr_sentinel

  local z_scope="${1:-}"
  local z_label="${2:-}"
  local z_file_path="${3:-}"
  test -n "${z_scope}" || buc_die "buv_render: scope required"
  test -n "${z_label}" || buc_die "buv_render: label required"

  local z_current_group=""
  local z_i
  local z_group=""
  local z_varname=""
  local z_type=""
  local z_desc=""

  echo ""
  zbuc_tint BUYC_BRIGHT_WHITE "${z_label}"; echo "${z_buym_format}"
  if test -n "${z_file_path}"; then
    zbuc_tint BUYC_GRAY "File: ${z_file_path}"; echo "  ${z_buym_format}"
  fi
  echo ""

  for z_i in "${!z_buv_scope_roll[@]}"; do
    test "${z_buv_scope_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_scope}" || continue

    z_group="${z_buv_group_roll[$z_i]}"
    z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_i]}"
    z_type="${z_buv_type_roll[$z_i]}"
    z_desc="${z_buv_desc_roll[$z_i]}"

    # Group transition ŌĆö close previous, open new
    if test "${z_group}" != "${z_current_group}"; then
      if test -n "${z_current_group}"; then
        bupr_section_end
      fi
      z_current_group="${z_group}"

      zbuv_group_gate_recite "${z_scope}" "${z_group}"
      if test -n "${ZBUV_GRP_GATE_VAR}"; then
        bupr_section_begin "${z_group}" "${ZBUV_GRP_GATE_VAR}" "${ZBUV_GRP_GATE_VAL}"
      else
        bupr_section_begin "${z_group}"
      fi
    fi

    zbuv_req_status "${z_i}"
    bupr_section_item "${z_varname}" "${z_type}" "${ZBUV_REQ_STATUS}" "${z_desc}"
  done

  # Close final group
  if test -n "${z_current_group}"; then
    bupr_section_end
  fi

toolUseResult.stdout:
=== buv_render body ===
buv_render() {
  zbuv_sentinel
  zbupr_sentinel

  local z_scope="${1:-}"
  local z_label="${2:-}"
  local z_file_path="${3:-}"
  test -n "${z_scope}" || buc_die "buv_render: scope required"
  test -n "${z_label}" || buc_die "buv_render: label required"

  local z_current_group=""
  local z_i
  local z_group=""
  local z_varname=""
  local z_type=""
  local z_desc=""

  echo ""
  zbuc_tint BUYC_BRIGHT_WHITE "${z_label}"; echo "${z_buym_format}"
  if test -n "${z_file_path}"; then
    zbuc_tint BUYC_GRAY "File: ${z_file_path}"; echo "  ${z_buym_format}"
  fi
  echo ""

  for z_i in "${!z_buv_scope_roll[@]}"; do
    test "${z_buv_scope_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_scope}" || continue

    z_group="${z_buv_group_roll[$z_i]}"
    z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_i]}"
    z_type="${z_buv_type_roll[$z_i]}"
    z_desc="${z_buv_desc_roll[$z_i]}"

    # Group transition ŌĆö close previous, open new
    if test "${z_group}" != "${z_current_group}"; then
      if test -n "${z_current_group}"; then
        bupr_section_end
      fi
      z_current_group="${z_group}"

      zbuv_group_gate_recite "${z_scope}" "${z_group}"
      if test -n "${ZBUV_GRP_GATE_VAR}"; then
        bupr_section_begin "${z_group}" "${ZBUV_GRP_GATE_VAR}" "${ZBUV_GRP_GATE_VAL}"
      else
        bupr_section_begin "${z_group}"
      fi
    fi

    zbuv_req_status "${z_i}"
    bupr_section_item "${z_varname}" "${z_type}" "${ZBUV_REQ_STATUS}" "${z_desc}"
  done

  # Close final group
  if test -n "${z_current_group}"; then
    bupr_section_end
  fi

content.text:
This changes the picture again ŌĆö and sharpens it. The actionability machinery already exists and is **good**, centralized in one engine.

## What `buv` already gives users

**validate** (`buv_report`) emits a structured line *per field*:
```
PASS  RBRN_MONIKER=tadmor [xname]
FAIL  RBRN_PORT=99999 [port]: RBRN_PORT value '99999' must be between 1 and 65535
SKIP  RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN (gated)
```
The reason strings (60+ of them) already name the field, state the constraint precisely, and echo the offending value ŌĆö *"must be at least N chars, got 'x' (3)"*, *"must match ^[a-z]... got 'x'"*. Secrets are redacted. This is genuinely fix-oriented.

**render** (`buv_render`) prints a grouped diagnostic view with a `File: <path>` header and per-field type/required/description.

**The load-bearing insight:** quality lives in `buv` (one place); every regime that routes through it inherits field-level actionable diagnostics. So the goal is *not* "write better messages" ŌĆö it's **guarantee every hand-authored regime fully inherits this engine, close the one gap the engine has, and lock it.**

The one real gap I can see: `buv_report` (validate) names the *field* but not the *file to edit* ŌĆö `buv_render` shows `File:` but the validate FAIL lines don't. And the deeper risk: a required field that isn't *enrolled* in the scope roll gets no PASS/FAIL line at all ŌĆö silent pass or downstream crash.

## The choices that get us there

**1. The actionability bar (definition of "good").** Where do we set it?
- *Floor (engine already meets):* every required field yields a FAIL naming field + constraint + value.
- *+ file pointer:* validate output tells the user *which file* to edit ŌĆö a one-place enhancement to `buv_report`, benefits every regime. **I recommend including this** ŌĆö it's the highest-leverage, smallest change, and closes the loop for users.

**2. Completeness ŌĆö the deepest lever.** How hard do we guarantee *every* required field is enrolled (so none can silently slip past validation)?
- *Light:* per regime, assert validate emits a FAIL for one representative bad value (proves it routes through `buv`).
- *Strong:* per regime, assert the enrolled var-set matches the fields the regime actually consumes (catches an un-enrolled field ŌĆö the silent hole). Higher value, needs a source of truth for "required fields."

**3. Engine vs per-regime.** Because quality is centralized, do we invest at the engine (broad reach, but touches shared `buv` in a release-finalize heat) or only at the regime/test layer? The file-pointer in #1 is the main engine candidate.

**4. Test assertion target.** Assert against the *structured* `FAILŌÉŻ<VARNAME>` token (stable contract, robust to reason rewording) ŌĆö and optionally also the reason substring (catches reason regressions, but brittle). **I recommend structured-token assertions** as the backbone.

**5. Regime scope.** Hand-authored set: `rbrn`, `rbrv`, `rbrr`, `rbrd`, `rbrp`, `rbrs`, BUK `burc`. In or out: `burn`/`burp` (hand-authored but station/remote-coupled)? Credentials (`rbro`/`rbra`) I'd keep out (installed, not authored).

**6. render readability.** Lock render too (assert it shows the file path + every enrolled field), or focus the pace on validate and treat render as already-good?

**7. Shape.** One pace, or a short sequence (engine file-pointer enhancement ŌåÆ per-regime enrollment-completeness tests)?

My instinct for the highest-merit version: **bar = floor + file-pointer (engine), completeness = strong where a field source-of-truth exists / light elsewhere, assert on structured tokens, scope the seven hand-authored regimes, lock render lightly.** That's load-bearing and squarely user-facing.

Which of these do you want to pin down, and where's your instinct different from mine ŌĆö especially on #2 (how hard we chase enrollment completeness) and #5 (scope)?

lastPrompt: #1 is definitely the intent.  What choices would help us reach this goal?

message.content: Here's a key litmus answer: I am not prepared to invest in bash sophistication to say print out in a pretty way when there are multiple violations- that is a tricky apparatus to test and either "a) all are good or b) at least item X is in violation" is sufficient.  Bash is horrid to test, so simplicity is key.  Also, a homogeneity between regimes is good, so user over time reifies RB's notion of a regime and its curation.  Thoughts?  Lets seek to do less than overhaul with this pace, I created it long ago when I realized the slow addition of regimes meant the regularity of treatment was lacking.  There are some wild subtleties: BCG is worth reading now, but the way that regimes are either singleton or manifold and the way that some regimes have special behaviors kept in _cli.sh files while the 'meat and potatoes' treatment in 'rbrX_regime.sh' are quite regular is a key pattern.  thoughts?

content.text: This is the real frame, and it humbles the pace correctly. Let me make sure I'm reading the pattern you're pointing at ŌĆö the singleton/manifold split and the regular `_regime.sh` vs. special `_cli.sh` division ŌĆö and pull the relevant BCG guidance, before I give you grounded thoughts.

content.content:
=== BCG: regime/singleton/manifold/kindle/enforce vocabulary ===
46:**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
50:External Code ŌåÆ CLI script ŌåÆ furnish() kindles ŌåÆ module functions
54:- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
55:- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
63:zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
79:**What can call kindle**:
81:- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
89:This makes the gateway role visible to `ls -l`: a workbench, testbench, or implementation module carrying `+x` is a bug ŌĆö direct execution bypasses the CLI's `furnish()` and the kindle graph it owns. Tabtargets in `tt/` and shipped tool binaries are not BCG modules and stand outside this rule.
93:**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
96:only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
97:function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
100:### Regime Module Archetype
102:A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields ŌĆö the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
106:| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
107:| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
108:| `z┬½prefix┬╗_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
109:| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
113:Furnish sequence: kindle ŌåÆ enforce ŌåÆ lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
115:**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
127:| `z┬½prefix┬╗_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z┬½PREFIX┬╗_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
128:| `z┬½prefix┬╗_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z┬½PREFIX┬╗_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
129:| `z┬½prefix┬╗_enforce`  | Implementation | `z┬½prefix┬╗_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
130:| `z┬½prefix┬╗_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
134:**Kindle constant**: Any variable ŌĆö internal (`Z┬½PREFIX┬╗_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`┬½PREFIX┬╗_SCREAMING_NAME`) ŌĆö defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function ŌĆö including enroll, setup, or helper functions ŌĆö may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
136:**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_┬½prefix┬╗_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
138:**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`┬½PREFIX┬╗_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z┬½PREFIX┬╗_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time ŌĆö **no runtime dependency, no `$(ŌĆ”)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${┬½PREFIX┬╗_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing ŌĆö no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling ŌĆö inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
140:**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z┬½PREFIX┬╗_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
148:| Public          | `┬½prefix┬╗_┬½command┬╗` | `z┬½prefix┬╗_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
149:| Internal helper | `z┬½prefix┬╗_┬½name┬╗`   | `z┬½prefix┬╗_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |
159:# shellcheck disable=SC2153  # kindle chain - per BCG
179:**Line-2 directive (REQUIRED for cross-module consumers).** Files that reference cross-module `Z*_/RB*_` readonly state ŌĆö full BCG modules and BCG-adjacent bootstrappers alike ŌĆö MUST include `# shellcheck disable=SC2153  # kindle chain - per BCG` on line 2, immediately after the shebang and before any other content. The coda wording is fixed; do not vary.
197:1. **Resolve the moorings location.** The consumer's configuration tree (launchers, regimes) lives under a moorings directory whose path the trampoline holds as a single hardcoded literal. This is the single point of customization at the tabtarget layer ŌĆö the parallel to BURC at the CLI layer. Move the moorings, edit one line; every tabtarget follows.
200:**The launcher name.** `BURD_LAUNCHER` is a bare launcher basename ŌĆö `launcher.<id>_workbench.sh`. Every launcher co-locates in the moorings launcher directory (`rbml_launchers/`), so the trampoline resolves the basename directly against that one directory and exec's it, forwarding the tabtarget basename and user args unchanged. `BURD_LAUNCHER` is not a private positional convention: it is a regime variable ŌĆö declared in `burd_regime`, allowlisted in `bud_dispatch` ŌĆö so the launcher identity travels the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value, and flows downstream unchanged.
235:  buc_doc_env "┬½PREFIX┬╗_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
242:  # Source regime files, if any
243:  source "${┬½PREFIX┬╗_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
245:  z┬½prefix┬╗_kindle
246:  z┬½prefix┬╗_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
267:  buc_doc_env "┬½PREFIX┬╗_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
271:  source "${┬½PREFIX┬╗_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"

Every launcher co-locates in the moorings launcher directory (`rbml_launchers/`), so the trampoline resolves the basename directly against that one directory and exec's it, forwarding the tabtarget basename and user args unchanged. `BURD_LAUNCHER` is not a private positional convention: it is a regime variable ŌĆö declared in `burd_regime`, allowlisted in `bud_dispatch` ŌĆö so the launcher identity travels the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value, and flows downstream unchanged.

content.text:
That output is the answer, and it largely vindicates "do less than overhaul." Let me give you grounded thoughts.

## What the data says

The `_regime.sh` "meat and potatoes" layer is **already remarkably regular**. All 14 regimes ŌĆö RBK and BUK alike ŌĆö show the identical archetype spine: `enroll` calls ŌåÆ exactly one `buv_scope_sentinel` ŌåÆ one `_enforce` ŌåÆ one `buv_vet`. That's the BCG Regime Module Archetype (┬¦100ŌĆō148) holding uniformly. The regularity you reached for has, in fact, largely arrived. So overhaul is unwarranted ŌĆö agreed.

The genuine residual asymmetries are narrow, and only two surfaced:

1. **The `readonly` lock step.** Only `burc_regime.sh` performs it in the regime module; the other 13 show none there. The archetype calls for "lock enrolled vars after enforce." Either the others lock in their `_cli` furnish, or they skip it ŌĆö worth one look, but this is a *judgment to flag*, probably not to fix in this pace (touching the lock on 13 modules is exactly the churn you want to avoid).

2. **Where manifold-ness lives.** BUK's manifold regimes (`burn`, `burp`) carry folio-conditional sourcing *in the regime module*; RBK's manifold regimes (`rbrn`, `rbrv`, `rbra`) keep it *in the `_cli` furnish* (regime-layer folioref=0). That's a real BUK-vs-RBK inhomogeneity in the singleton/manifold treatment ŌĆö but it may be load-bearing (different dispatch contexts), so again: flag, don't churn.

## Where the homogeneity actually pays the user

Not in the engine ŌĆö in the **test catalogue**. The existing `regime-validation` fixture already embodies precisely the binary bar you described: write a baseline config (ŌåÆ all PASS), mutate one field (ŌåÆ that field's FAIL), assert the *verdict* (`expect_pass` boolean). No pretty-printing, no multi-violation apparatus. That harness is the simplicity you want, and it already exists.

But it only covers four regimes (`rbrn`, `rbrr`, `rbrd`, `rbrv`). The other ten (`rbrp`, `rbrs`, `rbro`, `rbra`, `burc`, `burd`, `bure`, `burn`, `burp`, plus the manifold variants) get no such uniform treatment. **That gap is the inhomogeneity a user feels** ŌĆö some regimes have a legible "here's it passing, here's it catching one error" statement, others don't.

## So my proposed pace shape ŌĆö minimal, homogeneity-serving

Extend the *same existing harness* to every regime it doesn't yet cover, parameterized only by singleton-vs-manifold (singleton: source baseline directly; manifold: the folio-discovery the harness already does for `rbrn`/`rbrv`). Each regime gets the identical two-shape treatment:
- baseline config ŌåÆ all PASS (verdict 0)
- one seeded violation ŌåÆ that field FAILs (verdict non-zero)

The test suite then *becomes* the uniform, legible statement of "what a regime is and how it's curated in RB" ŌĆö which is the reification you created the pace for. Bash stays untested-as-prose; we assert only the binary verdict the harness already speaks.

It targets the regular `_regime.sh`/`enforce` layer (via the probate path), so the special `_cli` behaviors stay out of scope ŌĆö tested separately only where they carry weight, like the existing depot-unmake refusal.

Two things I'd want your call on: **(a)** whether the lock-step and manifold-location asymmetries above are load-bearing (flag-only) or you want them homogenized too ŌĆö I lean flag-only to honor "less than overhaul"; **(b)** whether `rbro`/`rbra` (credentials, not hand-authored) belong in the uniform catalogue at all, or stay out as you hinted earlier.

What's your read?

content.text:
Confirmed the full mechanism ŌĆö and it's exactly the shape your constraints want:

**The test logic is Rust.** Theurge (Rust) holds the baseline config as a string constant, applies a one-line override, writes a temp `.env`, then generates a tiny bash script ŌĆö `source buv; source the regime module; zbuv_kindle; <probate_fn> <envfile>` ŌĆö runs it, and asserts **only on exit code** against an `expect_ok` boolean. Pure binary verdict.

That dissolves your "bash is horrid to test" concern cleanly:
- The catalogue, the baselines, the mutations, the assertions all live in **typed Rust** ŌĆö testable, no bash test framework.
- **Bash is the system-under-test**, invoked as a subprocess. The only thing asserted is its exit code.
- The sole bash-side requirement per regime is a thin **`z┬½prefix┬╗_probate`** entry in `_regime.sh` ŌĆö source env ŌåÆ kindle ŌåÆ enforce ŌåÆ nonzero on failure. That's regular, archetype-shaped work, not logic-in-bash.

So extending the homogeneous catalogue is **mostly Rust**: add a baseline constant + two cases per regime. The bash delta is only "does each `_regime.sh` already expose the probate entry?" ŌĆö and that's itself a homogeneity check worth making explicit.

content.text:
Here's the precise delta ŌĆö and it answers "how much bash do we actually touch":

**The probate hook is the homogeneity gap itself.** Only the four regimes already in the catalogue expose a `┬½prefix┬╗_probate` entry (`rbrd`, `rbrn`, `rbrr`, `rbrv`). The other **ten have none**: `rbra`, `rbro`, `rbrp`, `rbrs` (RBK) and `burc`, `burd`, `bure`, `burn`, `burp`, `burs` (BUK).

So the pace is two regular, bounded pieces:

- **Bash side (small, archetype-regular):** add a thin `┬½prefix┬╗_probate` entry to each uncovered regime module ŌĆö source env ŌåÆ kindle ŌåÆ enforce ŌåÆ nonzero on failure, mirroring the four that exist. Note the existing convention is *unprefixed* `rbrX_probate`. This is itself the reification you want: every regime gains the identical testability hook, so "a regime" now uniformly *means* "has a probate." Manifold is already proven ŌĆö `rbrn`/`rbrv` are manifold and their probate handles folio.

- **Rust side (the bulk, and it's the testable part):** per regime, a `RBTDRM_PROBATE_*` const, a baseline config const, and two cases ŌĆö baselineŌåÆall-PASS, one-seeded-violationŌåÆthat-field-FAILs ŌĆö copying the existing shape.

**Judgment calls this surfaces:**
1. **`burd`** is the dispatch regime (BURD_* internals), not user-authored config ŌĆö I'd exclude it from the "hand-authored regime" catalogue.
2. **`rbro`/`rbra`** are installed credentials, not hand-authored ŌĆö you hinted they're out. They *can* get a probate + synthetic baseline for homogeneity, but it's awkward (credential-shaped values). Your call whether catalogue-homogeneity or authored-only wins.
3. The **lock-step** and **manifold-location** asymmetries from before stay flag-only (no churn).

That leaves a clean in-scope set of roughly seven genuinely hand-authored regimes (`rbrp`, `rbrs`, `burc`, `bure`, `burn`, `burp`, `burs`), each getting the identical probate + baseline + two-case treatment.

Does that sizing match your intent ŌĆö and how do you want to rule on `burd` and the credential pair before I draft the reslated docket?

=== rbrr_probate (singleton) ===
rbrr_probate() {
  local -r z_file="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_file}" || buc_die "rbrr_probate: regime file argument required"
  source "${z_file}"  || buc_die "rbrr_probate: cannot source ${z_file}"
  zrbrr_kindle
  zrbrr_enforce
}

  local -r z_file="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_file}" || buc_die "rbrn_probate: regime file argument required"
  source "${z_file}"  || buc_die "rbrn_probate: cannot source ${z_file}"
  zrbrn_kindle
  zrbrn_enforce
}

content.text:
Both probate functions are tiny and identical in shape, and there's a doc comment that states the intent outright. That's enough to explain it properly.

## The gestalt of `_probate`

**The problem it solves.** You want to test a regime's *validation logic* ŌĆö "does it reject a bad config with an actionable failure?" ŌĆö and you want that test in Rust (theurge), where it's typed and pleasant. But the real validation lives in bash, and normally you can only reach it by running the whole stack: tabtarget ŌåÆ launcher ŌåÆ workbench dispatch ŌåÆ `_cli.sh` furnish (which kindles the entire module graph) ŌåÆ `_validate` ŌåÆ `buv_report`. And that stack sources the config from a *fixed live path*. You can't easily say "validate *this* hand-crafted broken file" without faking the live location and dragging in all of dispatch.

**What probate is.** A deliberate, minimal **test seam** planted in the regular `_regime.sh` layer. Its entire body:

```bash
rbrr_probate() {
  local -r z_file="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_file}" || buc_die "... file argument required"
  source "${z_file}"  || buc_die "... cannot source ${z_file}"
  zrbrr_kindle
  zrbrr_enforce
}
```

"Source *an arbitrary file I hand you*, then run the real kindleŌåÆenforce chain against it, and die on first fault." The doc comment says it plainly: *"Test-facing contract surface: theurge drives synthetic-malformed regime files through this without reaching module internals."*

**The metaphor.** Probate is the legal proving of a will's validity. Here it adjudicates a config file: hand it a candidate, it returns a verdict ŌĆö exit 0 (valid) or nonzero (a field failed enforce). That exit code *is* the binary "all-good OR item-X-violated" bar you described.

**Why it's shaped exactly this way ŌĆö three properties carry the weight:**

1. **It tests the real thing.** Probate calls the *production* `kindle` and `enforce` ŌĆö the same code a user hits. The test isn't a reimplementation of the validation rules; it exercises them. If `enforce` is wrong, the test sees what the user sees.

2. **Uniform signature ŌåÆ one generic harness.** Every probate takes `(file) ŌåÆ exit code`, nothing else. That's why the Rust side (`rbtdrf_run_probate`) is a single function parameterized by regime name. The uniformity is the whole point: it's what lets the catalogue be homogeneous. Notice the singleton (`rbrr`) and manifold (`rbrn`) probate bodies are *identical* ŌĆö the singleton/manifold difference is absorbed by the harness (which instance's file to feed), not by probate itself. That's cleaner than I implied earlier.

3. **It's a narrow seam, not a wide door.** It bypasses `_cli` furnish and dispatch on purpose ŌĆö it tests *only* kindleŌåÆenforce, which is precisely the BCG archetype's "ironclad gate" and precisely the user-facing guarantee (bad field ŌåÆ caught). It does *not* test render, the CLI's special commands, or dispatch. Those are out of its remit by design.

**The honest cost ŌĆö name it.** Probate is test-only code living in a production module. A purist reading of load-bearing-complexity winces: the module carries a function that exists solely so theurge can reach it. The justification your predecessor was (implicitly) making: the alternative ŌĆö duplicating each regime's validation in Rust, or spinning up full dispatch with a faked live path ŌĆö is worse. A one-line seam that runs the genuine enforce chain is the cheapest honest way to test real bash from Rust. But it *is* test infrastructure with a foothold in the module, and that's the tradeoff you're being asked to extend tenfold.

**Why it matters for this pace.** "Universalize probate across the file-backed regimes" is, in gestalt, *"make every user-facing regime equally provable."* That's the reification you're after ŌĆö "a regime in RB" comes to mean, uniformly, "a typed config file with a kindleŌåÆenforce gate you can put on trial." The ten regimes lacking probate are the ones that can't yet be tried; the homogeneity gap and the test-coverage gap are the same gap.

## Closing your litmus question

Yes ŌĆö "has a file manifestation" is a solid, objective line, and it's better than my "hand-authored" phrasing. The verification confirms **`bure` is the clear sideways one** ŌĆö its own comment says *"BURE is ambient ŌĆö no env file to source, variables already in environment."* It's an internal boundary contract (the environment hand-off), never a file a user mis-edits, so relax it. **`burd`** is dispatch plumbing ŌĆö it references *other* regimes' file paths but isn't itself a user-authored file; sideways too.

And a bonus the file-litmus unlocks: the "uncommitted file" worry I raised earlier (`rbrs`, `burs`, `rbro`, `rbra`) **evaporates** ŌĆö probate is fed a *synthetic* baseline the harness writes to a temp dir, never the live committed file. So any file-backed regime is testable regardless of whether its real file is committed. The only true exclusions are the file-less sideways regimes (`bure`, `burd`).

### BURC (burc_regime.sh) ŌĆö Configuration regime (project-wide singleton)

  - Scope token passed: `BURC`

- **Custom enforce logic beyond `buv_vet`**: None. Enforce at lines 78ŌĆō82 only calls `zburc_sentinel` and `buv_vet BURC`. No format checks or cross-field validation.

  1. `BURC_STATION_FILE` (1ŌĆō512 chars, string)
  2. `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` (1ŌĆō128 chars, string)
  3. `BURC_TABTARGET_DELIMITER` (1ŌĆō1 chars, string ŌĆö single char)

  6. `BURC_MANAGED_KITS` (1ŌĆō512 chars, string)
  7. `BURC_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` (1ŌĆō512 chars, string)

  10. `BURC_LOG_EXT` (1ŌĆō16 chars, xname ŌĆö same pattern)
  11. `BURC_BUK_DIR` (derived from BURC_TOOLS_DIR at kindle time, line 36; 1ŌĆō256 chars, string ŌĆö optional in user .env, auto-populated)

Survey complete ŌĆö all 12 file-based regimes profiled. Here's the synthesis and a proposed reslate for your review.

## What the survey shows

The 4 covered regimes (`rbrr`, `rbrd`, `rbrn`, `rbrv`) are the reference. The 8 lacking a probate, with the wrinkles that matter for authoring baseline + violation cases:

| Regime | S/M | Custom enforce (violation target) | Baseline wrinkle |
|---|---|---|---|
| `rbrp` | singleton | payor-project regex, billing/oauth formats | **enforce calls `zrbgc_sentinel`** ŌåÆ probate must kindle RBGC |
| `rbrs` | singleton | none | 3 plain strings ŌĆö trivial |
| `rbro` | role-scoped | none | 2 credential strings; mirror existing `rbro_load()` |
| `rbra` | role-scoped | role enum, SA-email suffix, PEM `BEGIN` check | synthetic creds; `RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY` is a `buv_secret` field |
| `burc` | singleton | none | ~10 fields, `BURC_BUK_DIR` derived at kindle |
| `burn` | node (manifold) | none | 2 fields (HOST, PLATFORM enum) ŌĆö trivial |
| `burp` | per-user (manifold) | none | 3 fields, SSH key *paths* (synthetic) |
| `burs` | singleton | `BURS_TINCTURE` regex | 3 fields |

**The structural confirmation that simplifies everything:** probate is *layer-uniform* ŌĆö it sources whatever file it's handed, kindles, enforces. Singleton-vs-manifold is irrelevant to probate (it only mattered to the live-render path we dropped). So all 8 get the **identical** probate shape; the *only* real variation is `rbrp`'s RBGC kindle dependency.

**A clean test-design rule falls out:** baseline ŌåÆ all-PASS; one seeded violation ŌåÆ that-field-FAIL (verdict only). Where a regime has a *custom* enforce check (`rbrd` joint-length, `rbrp`/`rbra` regexes, `burs` tincture), aim the violation there ŌĆö those bespoke checks are the highest regression risk and most regime-specific. Otherwise, unset a required field (the existing `rbrn_missing_moniker` pattern).

## Three rulings I need from you

1. **Credentials `rbro`/`rbra` ŌĆö in or out?** Your file-litmus pulls them *in* (they're file-backed; a malformed installed credential *is* a real user-facing failure; synthetic baselines are trivial). Earlier you leaned out ("installed, not authored"). I now recommend **in** ŌĆö homogeneity wins and the synthetic baseline is cheap. Your call.
2. **Relabel.** "prerelease-regime-render-sweep" no longer fits ŌĆö render is out, this is validation-probate. Propose **`regime-probate-homogeneity`** (or your preference).
3. **Render confirmed out of scope?** `buv_render` already gives the structured, file-pointing diagnostic view ŌĆö I see no user-facing guarantee a render smoke would add.

## Proposed reslated docket (draft)

> **## Character**
> Uniform mechanical replication across regimes, gated on one test-design rule and a few flagged per-regime wrinkles.
>
> **## Goal**
> Make every file-based regime uniformly *provable*: each gains a `┬½prefix┬╗_probate` test seam and a matching theurge fast-fixture case pair, so a misconfiguration of *any* regime is caught with the same actionable enforce verdict. Reifies "a regime" as "a typed config with a kindleŌåÆenforce gate you can put on trial."
>
> **## Scope**
> The file-based regimes lacking a probate: `rbrp`, `rbrs`, `rbro`, `rbra` (`Tools/rbk/*_regime.sh`) and `burc`, `burn`, `burp`, `burs` (`Tools/buk/*_regime.sh`). `rbrr`/`rbrd`/`rbrn`/`rbrv` are the reference. Sideways file-less regimes (`bure` ambient, `burd` dispatch) are out ŌĆö their failures are not user-config failures.
>
> **## The two parts**
> - *Bash:* add `┬½prefix┬╗_probate` mirroring `rbrr_probate` (source handed file ŌåÆ kindle ŌåÆ enforce). `rbrp` must additionally kindle RBGC (payor-regex dep); `rbro`/`rbra`/`burp` take synthetic non-secret baselines.
> - *Rust (theurge fast fixture):* per regime, a baseline-valid case + one seeded-violation case, verdict-only. Aim the violation at the custom enforce check where one exists, else an unset required field.
>
> **## Out of scope**
> `render` (already structured/actionable via `buv_render`); the lock-step asymmetry (only BURC locks) and BUK-vs-RBK manifold-location asymmetry ŌĆö flag if seen, do not churn.
>
> **## Done**
> Every file-based regime has a probate and a baseline+violation pair in the fast fixture; `rbw-tt` and the fast suite green.
>
> **## References**
> `Tools/rbk/rbrr_regime.sh` (reference probate + its test-seam doc comment) ┬Ę `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` (`rbtdrf_run_probate` harness, existing `rv_` cases) ┬Ę `Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh` (`buv_report`/`buv_vet`) ┬Ę BCG "Regime Module Archetype".

Give me the three rulings (and any edits to the draft) and I'll `jjx_redocket` + `jjx_relabel` accordingly.

content.text:
Reslate landed. ŌéóAUAAh is now **regime-probate-homogeneity** with the new docket.

## Summary of the reframe

The pace went from a stale "invoke every render/validate/info in the fast fixture" (opus-4.7 framing, partly incoherent ŌĆö info commands hit GCP, can't live in the no-dependency tier) to a tight, user-value-anchored target:

**Make every file-based regime uniformly *provable*.** Each of the 8 uncovered regimes (`rbrp`, `rbrs`, `rbro`, `rbra`, `burc`, `burn`, `burp`, `burs`) gains a thin `┬½prefix┬╗_probate` seam mirroring the 4 that exist, plus a baseline-valid + one-seeded-violation case pair in theurge ŌĆö verdict-only, binary, no bash-side test apparatus.

The work that got us here:
- **File-manifestation litmus** drew the scope line cleanly ŌĆö `bure`/`burd` (sideways, file-less) out; credentials in (a malformed installed credential *is* a user-facing failure, synthetic baselines are cheap).
- **The probate-is-layer-uniform insight** collapsed the singleton/manifold complexity ŌĆö probate sources whatever file it's handed, so all 8 take the identical shape; only `rbrp`'s RBGC kindle dep varies.
- **The test-design rule** ŌĆö aim the seeded violation at each regime's custom enforce check where one exists, else an unset field ŌĆö keeps the catalogue homogeneous while exercising the highest-risk bespoke logic.

The reslate diff is clean and the relabel committed (`fa0b836`). The pace is ready to mount when you are ŌĆö it's mostly Rust (the testable part), with a small, archetype-regular bash delta. Want me to mount it now, or leave it slated?
